1: vayehi bishnat arba leDaryavesh hamelek haya davar YHWH el Zekharyahu bearba'ah lachodesh hateviit bekhislev: 'In the fourth year of King Darius, the word of the LORD came to Zechariah on the fourth day of the ninth month, which is Chislev.' The date formula (December 518 BCE) — two years after the eight night visions (520-519 BCE) and while the temple construction continues. The occasion is a question from Bethel about the fourth-month and fifth-month fasts that commemorated the siege and destruction of Jerusalem. The question initiates an extended response (chs 7-8) about the meaning of religious observance and the proper heart of covenant religion.
2: vayishlach Beit El Sar'etzer veRegem Melekh va'anashav lechalot et penei YHWH: 'Now Bethel had sent Sharezer and Regem-melech and their men to entreat the favor of the LORD.' The delegation from Bethel (Beit El = house of God, ironically: the city associated with Jeroboam's golden calves, 1 Kgs 12:29) comes to inquire of YHWH. The proper response to questions about religious practice is to seek YHWH's will — a positive impulse behind the question that YHWH's response will deepen and redirect.
3: leimor el hakkohanim asher leVeit YHWH tzvaot ve'el hanevi'im leimor ha'evkeh bachodesh hachameshy hinazer kehasher asiti zeh kammeh shanim: 'To ask the priests of the house of the LORD of hosts and the prophets, Should I weep and abstain in the fifth month, as I have done for so many years?' The fifth-month fast (tzom hachamishi) commemorated the burning of the temple and destruction of Jerusalem in Ab/Tevet of 586 BCE (2 Kgs 25:8-9; Jer 52:12-13). The question: now that the temple is being rebuilt, is this fast still required? The question is legitimate and practical, but YHWH's response redirects it to a deeper question about the nature of all religious observance.
4: vayehi davar YHWH tzvaot elai leimor: 'Then the word of the LORD of hosts came to me.' The prophetic formula introduces YHWH's extended response to the Bethel delegation. Rather than simply answering yes or no about the fast, YHWH uses the question as an occasion for a comprehensive teaching about covenant faithfulness.
5: emor el kol am ha'aretz ve'el hakkohanim leimor ki tztzmtem vesfod bachameshy uvashvi'i vezeh shiv'im shanah ha'ani tzamtem tzom tzoniy li: 'Say to all the people of the land and the priests: When you fasted and mourned in the fifth month and in the seventh month, for these seventy years, was it for me that you fasted?' The penetrating question cuts to the motivational core: ha'ani tzamtem tzom tzoniy li (was it really for me that you fasted for me?). The repeated pronoun li (for me) emphasizes the point: fasting done for God vs. fasting done for human emotional release or social obligation. YHWH challenges the assumption that religious observance is automatically done 'for him' — it may in fact be for oneself.
6: vechekhalachem vashtochem halo atem haokhlim vehem shottim: 'And when you eat and when you drink, do you not eat for yourselves and drink for yourselves?' The logic is extended: if your eating and drinking is for yourselves, so is your fasting. Both religious observance (fasting) and ordinary life (eating/drinking) are evaluated by the same criterion: is it done unto YHWH or for oneself? The NT form: whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God (1 Cor 10:31). The problem is not fasting per se but fasting disconnected from covenant relationship with YHWH.
7: halo et haddevarim asher qara YHWH beyadh hanevi'im harishonim bihyot Yerushalayim yoshevet ushleivah ve'areha saviveiha vehanegev veshefelah yoshevet: 'Were not these the words that the LORD proclaimed by the former prophets, when Jerusalem was inhabited and prosperous, and her cities around her, and the Negeb and the Shephelah were inhabited?' The 'former prophets' again — YHWH's question points back to the pre-exilic prophetic tradition. The same ethical demands were given when Jerusalem was prosperous and populated; their rejection led to the exile. The current community, returning from exile, is asked: will you repeat the fathers' pattern or learn from it?
8: vayehi davar YHWH el Zekharyahu leimor: 'And the word of the LORD came to Zechariah, saying.' The formal oracle introduction marks a new element in YHWH's response: the positive content of what covenant faithfulness actually requires. Verses 9-10 contain the three-part ethical summary that is the OT's most concentrated statement of social covenant obligation.
9: koh amar YHWH tzvaot mishpat emet shafetu vecheced verachamim asu ish et achiv: 'Thus says the LORD of hosts: Render true judgments, show kindness and mercy to one another.' The three covenant-ethics: (1) mishpat emet (true/faithful justice — righteous judgment in the courts); (2) chesed (H2617 — steadfast covenant love, loyal kindness — in human relationships); (3) rachamim (H7356 — compassion, tender mercy — toward the vulnerable). These are the three dimensions of the OT's social ethic, corresponding to the three levels of human relationship: legal-formal (justice in courts), covenant-communal (loyal love between partners), and compassionate (tender care for the weak). Micah 6:8's do justice, love kindness, and walk humbly with your God is the same three-fold structure.
10: ve'almanah veyatom ger ve'ani al ta'ashoqu verah ish achiv al tacheshvu bilvavchem: 'Do not oppress the widow, the fatherless, the sojourner, or the poor, and let none of you devise evil against another in your heart.' The four protected classes — widow, orphan, sojourner, poor — are the most vulnerable members of covenant society, those without natural protectors. Their protection is the litmus test of covenant faithfulness throughout the Mosaic law (Exod 22:21-24; Deut 14:29; 24:17-22) and the prophets (Isa 1:17; Jer 7:6; Amos 5:12; Mal 3:5). The final prohibition — 'let none devise evil in his heart' — addresses the internal disposition before the external act: the heart's intentions, not just the visible behavior, are under covenant obligation.
11: vayema'anu lehaqshiv vayitenu katef soreref ve'ozneihem hikbidu mishmo'a: 'But they refused to pay attention and turned a stubborn shoulder and stopped their ears that they might not hear.' The fathers' response to the same prophetic message: refusal and defiance. Katef soreref (H5637 — a stubbornly turned shoulder) is the image of an ox or donkey that resists the yoke by turning its shoulder away. The stopping of ears (hikbidu mishmo'a = they made their ears heavy/dull from hearing) is the same hardening that Isaiah describes (Isa 6:10: make their ears heavy and their eyes shut). The physical metaphors for spiritual resistance are vivid: not passive ignorance but active refusal.
12: velibam samu shamir mishmo'a et haTorah ve'et hadevarim asher shalach YHWH tzvaot berucham el yad hanevi'im harishonim vayehi qetzef gadol me'et YHWH tzvaot: 'They made their hearts diamond-hard lest they should hear the law and the words that the LORD of hosts had sent by his Spirit through the former prophets. Therefore great anger came from the LORD of hosts.' Lev shamir (a heart of diamond/flint, H8068 — the hardest known stone in the ancient world, used for engraving) — the ultimate hardening of the heart against YHWH's word. The shamir stone is so hard it can cut other stones; used metaphorically for a heart that repels all impression from YHWH's word. The great anger (qetzef gadol) that resulted was the exile: YHWH's word sent through the prophets, refused, led to the covenant curse.
13: vayehi ka'asher qara velo shame'u ken yiqre'u velo eshmea amar YHWH tzvaot: 'As I called, and they would not hear, so they called, and I would not hear, says the LORD of hosts.' The talio principle of divine-human reciprocity: they did not hear my call → I will not hear their call. The symmetry is judicial: the same deafness they applied to YHWH's prophets, YHWH applies to their prayers in crisis. This is the prophetic warning pattern: Prov 1:24-28 uses the same structure (I called and you refused ... then you will call and I will not answer). The exile was the actualization of this reciprocal deafness.
14: va'esa'rem al kol hagoyim asher lo yeda'um veha'aretz neshammah achareihem me'over ume'ashev vayasimu eretz chemdah lishemamah: 'And I scattered them with a whirlwind among all the nations that they had not known. Thus the land they left was desolate, so that no one went to and fro, and the pleasant land was made desolate.' YHWH scattered them (asa'rem bise'arah = with a storm-wind/whirlwind I scattered them) among nations they had not known. The pleasant land (eretz chemdah, H2532 — the desirable, beautiful land of Canaan) was made desolate: the land that was gift became emptied of its people. The historical reality of 586-539 BCE is the theological application of vv11-13's analysis of covenant hardening. The chapter ends with desolation — setting up ch8's mirror reversal with restoration promises.
